fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Flexi Cap Fund Direct Plan -Growth
fund.fund_house: Bank of India Investment Managers Private Limited

allocations.rationale: Your preferred Flexi Cap entry point. BoI Flexi Cap has delivered 19.5% CAGR over 5 years with impressive alpha of 18.3% — beating peers through active selection across the cap spectrum. At 1% fee, it's competitive. This fund gives you the flexible allocation you appreciate: it can swing between large and small caps based on valuations, rather than being locked into a rigid index formula. Since you read factsheets the way others read novels, you'll note its Sharpe ratio of 1.1 reflects excellent risk-adjusted returns. The small AUM (₹515 Cr) keeps it nimble without diluting performance.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Large cap anchor for stability and liquidity. Nippon India Large Cap's 17.7% 5-year CAGR with 0.98 beta (nearly market-matched) offers consistent outperformance without excessive risk. Its tight tracking error (3.16%) and seasoned management (18 years old, ₹16.6k Cr AUM) give you a rock-solid core. At 22 years to retirement, you need this ballast — it will cushion downturns and compound steadily. The 1% fee is standard for actively managed large caps.

fund.name: Edelweiss Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap pick for long-term growth acceleration. Edelweiss Mid Cap delivered 21.7% CAGR over 5 years with alpha of 15.17% — evidence of genuine stock-picking skill. The 1.15% fee is standard for mid-caps and justified by the outperformance. Its Sharpe ratio of 1.15 is excellent (higher than many large-cap peers), meaning you get more return per unit of risk. At age 36, you have 22 years: mid-caps historically return ~13-15% over long periods, and this fund beats that mark significantly. A ₹18k/month SIP here seeds meaningful compounding in this higher-growth segment.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Passive mid-cap diversification. The Nifty Midcap 150 index at 0.2% fee offers a mathematically pure mid-cap play — no manager skill required, no alpha decay risk. It's returned 18.7% over 5 years, tracking the broader 150-company midcap universe. Use this for 15% (via SIP only) to balance the active Edelweiss Mid Cap pick above. This dual approach — active + passive in the same segment — gives you both a high-conviction active bet AND index-like diversification. The ultra-low 0.2% fee (40x cheaper than active mid-caps) also protects you against fee drag if the manager underperforms.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC Limited

allocations.rationale: Debt allocation for downside cushion and emergency liquidity. Medium-term debt is slab-taxed (you'll pay 30% on gains), so a 9.6% pre-tax return becomes ~6.7% post-tax — but still beats FDs and guarantees capital stability. AB Medium Term has returned 12.7% over 5 years (post-tax: ~9%), with Sharpe ratio of 2.24 (exceptional risk-adjusted return). This 10% allocation gives you ₹9k/month passive income, plus a steady ₹250k lumpsum deployment. With ₹2.5Cr existing corpus, this helps you build the ₹1Cr emergency buffer (goal) without over-exposure to equity volatility in your safety bucket.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Account Setup (2 hours) — Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU. Link your bank account for SIPs. This is tax-efficient vs. regular (commissioned) plans.

Step 2: Lumpsum Deployment (Week 1) — You have ₹2.5Cr existing corpus. From this, invest ₹2.125Cr across the five funds as per the lumpsum amounts above. Leave ₹375Cr aside in a liquid overnight fund for your emergency buffer — this earns ~5.5% and is entirely tax-efficient.

Step 3: Start Monthly SIPs (Immediate) — Set up five SIP mandates for ₹90k/month total: ₹27k to BoI Flexi Cap, ₹22.5k to Nippon Large Cap, ₹18k to Edelweiss Mid Cap, ₹13.5k to Motilal Oswal Midcap 150 Index, ₹9k to AB Medium Term Debt. Configure SIPs for the 5th of each month. All direct plans use growth option.

Step 4: Annual SIP Step-Up (Year 2 onwards) — Increase each SIP by 10% every January. This mimics your career progression and keeps pace with inflation.

Step 5: First Month Checklist — Confirm all five SIPs are active. Download factsheets for each fund. Check your portfolio via the app; it should show ₹2.125Cr in equities/debt and ₹375Cr in liquid. Note: NAV updates after market close (~4:30 PM IST).
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Confirm all five SIPs have gone through (₹270k total deployed). Check if any fund has not credited units.
After 6 months: Review each fund's 6-month performance. If any fund underperforms peers by >3%, flag for analysis — but don't panic-switch; one quarter is noise.
After 12 months: Run full-year performance check. Portfolio should have ≥12% CAGR (base case). Review 3-year track records. Increase all SIPs by 10% as planned.
After 3 years (age 39): Check if equity allocation has drifted >5% from 80%. If so, shift 5-10% to debt to rebalance. Expected portfolio value: ~₹5.5-6.5Cr.

Annually (every Dec): Print year-end statement and file with tax records. Email factsheets to CA 30 days before ITR filing. If any fund chronically underperforms (Sharpe ratio dropped >0.2), consider a switch.
Red flags to act immediately: Fund closes; fund manager leaves and replacement is weaker; fund AUM falls below ₹50Cr; major life change (job loss, large liability, relocation).
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Target allocation: 80% Equity + 10% Debt. Rebalance once a year (December) or when any category drifts >5% from target. If equity booms to 85%, sell ₹150L from your highest-performing equity fund and buy ₹150L of debt. Sell debt first (already slab-taxed). Sell equities only after holding >1 year (you'll qualify for 12.5% LTCG on gains >₹1.25L). If you get a windfall (₹50L bonus), invest 80% in equity (split equally among your three active equity picks) and 20% in debt. Do not overweight the best-performing fund of the moment.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
## Dimension-by-Dimension Analysis

### passive_instrument_fraction = 0.15
Within the equity portfolio (75% allocation), only the Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund is a true passive instrument. This represents 15% of total allocation (5% SIP-only) or 20% of the equity sleeve (15/75). The remaining 60% of portfolio is allocated to actively managed equities: BoI Flexi Cap (30%), Nippon Large Cap (25%), and Edelweiss Mid Cap (20%). Score reflects ~15% passive equity fraction at portfolio level.

### turnover_score = 0.75
The plan demonstrates a **buy-and-hold ethos with structured, infrequent rebalancing**, scoring it as quite passive (0.75). Evidence:
- Explicit annual rebalancing ("Rebalance once a year in December, or when any category drifts >5%")
- No tactical sector rotation, momentum trading, or monthly adjustments mentioned
- SIP step-up is systematic (10% annually), not opportunistic
- Review checkpoints are infrequent (3, 6, 12 months, then annually) and focused on monitoring rather than active trading
- Rebalancing rationale emphasizes tax efficiency and discipline ("sell equities only after holding >1 year")
- 22-year time horizon reinforces long-term holding conviction
Not quite 1.0 (buy-and-hold perfection) because the plan does recommend annual rebalancing and the 3-month/6-month checkpoints introduce some active review cadence.

### cost_emphasis_score = 0.45
The plan shows **moderate cost awareness but does not lead with cost as primary criterion**. Evidence:
- Direct plan selection is explicitly tax-efficient ("This is tax-efficient vs. regular (commissioned) plans")
- Rationale acknowledges fees (1%, 1.15%, 0.2%) but does not emphasize minimizing them as the dominant criterion
- Fund selection is driven primarily by **outperformance and alpha**: "BoI Flexi Cap has delivered 19.5% CAGR... with impressive alpha of 18.3%", "Edelweiss Mid Cap delivered 21.7% CAGR with alpha of 15.17%"
- Passive index fund (0.2% fee) is justified as "diversification" and "risk hedge", not as cost savings strategy
- Actively managed funds are chosen for skill, not rejected for higher fees
- Medium-term debt is slab-taxed but accepted because it "beats FDs and guarantees capital stability"
Score 0.45 reflects reasonable cost consciousness without cost-first decision-making.

### research_vs_cost_score = 0.35
The plan demonstrates **substantial fund-level research and active conviction**, scoring it as having genuine research orientation (0.35, closer to active). Evidence:
- Each active fund is analysed for: specific alpha metrics (18.3% for BoI, 15.17% for Edelweiss, 4.27% for Nippon), Sharpe ratios (1.1, 1.15, 0.63), fund manager tenure and AUM ("18 years old, ₹16.6k Cr AUM"), and track records over multiple horizons (1y, 3y, 5y)
- Rationale includes qualitative assessment: "gives you the flexible allocation you appreciate", "rock-solid core", "genuine stock-picking skill"
- The dual active+passive mid-cap approach reflects explicit research judgment ("active + passive in the same segment gives you both a high-conviction active bet")
- Debt fund is analysed for post-tax returns and Sharpe ratio (2.24)
- No reliance on "just buy the index" philosophy; instead, conviction-driven picks with manager credibility
However, not 0.0 because the plan does allocate 15% to a broad Nifty Midcap 150 index (passive diversification) and emphasizes systematic SIP over individual security picking.

### time_horizon_alignment_score = 0.85
The plan is **explicitly long-term oriented and highly aligned with a 22-year retirement horizon**. Evidence:
- "At age 36, you have 22 years: mid-caps historically return ~13-15% over long periods"
- "At 22 years to retirement, you need this ballast — it will cushion downturns and compound steadily"
- Annual SIP step-up mimics career progression, reinforcing decades-long compounding
- Rebalancing is annual, not quarterly or monthly
- Review checkpoints focus on long-term performance, not tactical windows
- Strategy explicitly discourages panic-selling ("one quarter is noise"; "don't panic-switch")
- 3-year review expects portfolio to reach ₹5.5–6.5Cr, illustrating multi-year compounding mindset
Not 1.0 because the plan does include 6-month and 12-month review checkpoints (some active oversight), and rebalancing does introduce occasional tactical moves.

### portfolio_activeness_score = 0.25
The portfolio is **genuinely active-oriented at the core but with passive hedge**, scoring 0.25 (moderately active, not passive). Evidence:
**Active components (75% of equity):**
- BoI Flexi Cap: Alpha 18.3%, beta 0.87, Sharpe 1.1 → genuine alpha generation with concentrated bets
- Edelweiss Mid Cap: Alpha 15.17%, beta 0.96, Sharpe 1.15 → strong active outperformance
- Nippon Large Cap: Alpha 4.27%, beta 0.98, Sharpe 0.63 → index-like beta but consistent positive alpha (seasoned fund)

All three actively managed funds show tracking error close to index (beta ≈ 0.87–0.98, not >1.2) and moderate Sharpe ratios, but the alpha profiles are genuinely positive and meaningful. These are not closet indexers.

**Passive component (15% of equity):**
- Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150: Beta 1.03, alpha 11.74%, 0.2% fee → pure index tracking by definition (despite the alpha, which reflects the index itself outperforming Nifty 50 in the measurement period)

**Debt (10%):**
- AB Medium Term: Category-based rationale, not Nifty 50-comparable. Treated as actively managed credit/duration fund (alpha 12.47% vs. Nifty 50 is noise). But functionally passive as a category.

**Overall portfolio activeness:** 75% actively managed with genuine alpha, 15% passive index. Weighted average is moderately active (0.25 reflects "active core with passive hedge" positioning).

## Composite APS Calculation
Unweighted average of six dimensions:
- (0.15 + 0.75 + 0.45 + 0.35 + 0.85 + 0.25) / 6 = 2.80 / 6 = **0.467**

**Interpretation:** This plan sits **slightly below the midpoint** on the Active-Passive spectrum, leaning **moderately toward active management**. It is neither a pure passive index strategy nor a deeply active stock-picker's playbook. Instead, it combines active conviction (three actively managed equity funds with proven alpha) with passive diversification (one index fund) and disciplined systematic execution (annual rebalancing, SIP step-ups, infrequent reviews). The plan suits a patient, research-informed investor with a long-term horizon who values manager skill and alpha but also respects the limits of active management (hence the index hedge).

pqs.reasoning:
## Score Breakdown

### Goal Alignment (0.82/1.0)
**Strengths:**
- The 80/10 equity-debt split is reasonable for a 36-year-old with 22-year horizon and moderate risk appetite, supporting long-term compounding toward the ₹8Cr retirement goal.
- Monthly SIPs (₹90k) plus step-up logic align with career progression and inflation management.
- Explicit emergency buffer via overnight fund (₹375Cr aside) directly addresses the ₹1Cr buffer goal.
- Children's education (12 & 15 years away) is implicitly covered by equity growth, though no specific goal-mapping or timeline-based bucketing is shown.

**Weaknesses:**
- No explicit calculation or projection linking the ₹2.125Cr lumpsum + ₹90k SIP + 12.6% base case to whether ₹8Cr retirement target is achievable. Plan states base case CAGR but doesn't show: (a) portfolio value at age 55, (b) shortfall/surplus, or (c) SIP adjustment if shortfall emerges.
- Children's education abroad is a major goal but receives no dedicated corpus, bucketing, or timeline. Plan treats all goals as a single "equity growth" problem.
- Missing: explicit mapping of the 10% debt allocation to the ₹1Cr emergency buffer. Is the ₹375Cr overnight fund + ₹250Cr debt lumpsum adequate? Plan doesn't verify.

**Overall:** Strong directional alignment but lacks granular goal-to-allocation bridge and CFP-style projections.

---

### Diversification (0.78/1.0)
**Strengths:**
- Good multi-cap spread: 30% Flexi Cap (flexible across cap spectrum) + 25% Large Cap + 20% Active Mid Cap + 15% Passive Mid Cap + 10% Debt.
- Fund-house diversity: 5 different AMCs (BoI, Nippon, Edelweiss, Motilal Oswal, AB Sunlife).
- Active + Passive hybrid within mid-caps (Edelweiss + Motilal index) reduces single-manager and style drift risk.
- Geographic implicit: Indian equity funds with some indirect global exposure through large-cap holdings (not explicit).

**Weaknesses:**
- **No international equity exposure:** Plan mentions investor "likes international diversification" but allocates 0% to foreign funds. This is a material omission given the 22-year horizon and goal of children's education abroad (likely USD-denominated liability).
- **Debt is domestic only:** No gold, REIT, or inflation-hedging assets.
- **Concentration in mid-caps:** 35% combined (20% active Edelweiss + 15% index Motilal) in a single cap segment; no small-cap fund despite 22-year horizon.
- **Flexi Cap + Large Cap + Mid Cap overlap:** While sectors differ, there's significant overlap in holdings (e.g., HDFC Bank, Reliance in all three equity funds). True diversification would add uncorrelated assets or geographies.

**Overall:** Reasonable for domestic Indian investor, but ignores stated preference for international exposure and lacks hedges against INR depreciation (relevant for education abroad goal).

---

### Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.80/1.0)
**Strengths:**
- 80/10 split is sound for moderate appetite at age 36 with 22-year horizon. Not aggressive, not conservative.
- Base case 12.6% CAGR is realistic given historical equity (~13-15%) and debt (~7-9%) returns. Bull (16.6%) and bear (8.6%) scenarios are reasonable.
- Debt cushion (10%) moderates downside. In a 30-40% equity crash, portfolio decline would be ~24-32%, recoverable within 3-5 years.
- Fund selection: mostly outperforming peers (Edelweiss Mid Cap +15.17% alpha, BoI Flexi +18.3% alpha, AB Debt +12.47% alpha). Historical Sharpe ratios are strong.

**Weaknesses:**
- **Alpha sustainability:** High alpha funds (BoI, Edelweiss) have short track records (inception 2020, 2007). BoI Flexi Cap is only 4 years old; its 18.3% alpha may reflect market tailwinds, not repeatable skill.
- **No downside stress test:** Plan doesn't address what happens if equity returns 6-8% CAGR instead of 12.6% (e.g., in a prolonged bear market, 2000-2003 or 2008-2009 scenario). Would the retirement goal be missed?
- **Volatility not modeled:** Plan cites Sharpe ratios but doesn't quantify portfolio volatility or max drawdown. Investor says "moderate" risk appetite — but is 20-25% annual volatility acceptable to him? Unknown.
- **SIP step-up assumption:** 10% annual increase assumes stable salary and no job loss / market downturn. In 2008-style crisis, investor might be forced to pause or reduce SIPs.

**Overall:** Reasonable allocation and realistic base returns, but lacks stress-testing and volatility transparency needed to confirm "moderate" fit.

---

### Internal Consistency (0.85/1.0)
**Strengths:**
- Clear narrative: flexi cap (high-conviction) + large cap (anchor) + mid caps (active + passive blend) + debt (stability) + emergency buffer (overnight fund). Rationale for each fund is coherent.
- Setup and review checkpoints are detailed and actionable (5-step process, quarterly reviews, red flags).
- Rebalancing rules are explicit: 80/10 target, ±5% drift triggers, LTCG tax awareness (hold >1 year before selling), preference to sell debt first.
- All SIPs total ₹90k/month, matching the monthly investible surplus. Lumpsums total ₹2.125Cr from ₹2.5Cr corpus, leaving ₹375Cr for emergency buffer — math checks out.
- Growth option (not dividend) is consistent with long-term compounding.

**Weaknesses:**
- **Debt allocation rationale mismatch:** Plan says 10% debt is for "downside cushion and emergency liquidity," but then separately allocates ₹375Cr to overnight fund for emergency buffer. So is the debt allocation (AB Medium Term) for downside cushion or income? This creates ambiguity. Post-tax return of debt (6.7%) is mentioned but not compared to overnight fund (5.5%) or FD equivalent.
- **International exposure contradiction:** Plan says investor "likes international diversification" (in the life_stage description) but allocates 0% internationally. This is a logical inconsistency — the rationale should explain why international is excluded (e.g., "currency risk, home bias justified by 22 years") but it doesn't.
- **SIP step-up assumption not explained:** Plan assumes 10% annual SIP increase but doesn't state what happens if salary stagnates or income drops. Is this assumption documented in the investor's salary growth expectations?

**Overall:** Strong internal logic, but three areas (debt purpose, international omission, SIP step-up assumption) create minor narrative breaks.

---

### Tax Efficiency (0.62/1.0) — **This is the key weakness**
**Critical Issues:**

1. **Debt Fund Choice in 30% Slab — Major Miss:**
   - Investor is in 30% tax bracket. AB Medium Term debt fund returns ~9.6% pre-tax, but after 30% slab tax, post-tax return is **~6.7%**.
   - Plan mentions "9.6% pre-tax becomes ~6.7% post-tax" but **does not justify why slab-taxed debt is optimal** for a 30% slab investor.
   - **Arbitrage funds** (debt ≥75%, quasi-equity tax treatment if structured as hybrid) or **Aggressive Hybrid funds** (≥65% equity, so equity-taxed at 12.5% LTCG or 20% STCG) would deliver similar risk profiles but at **much better post-tax returns** for a 30%-slab investor.
   - Example: An arbitrage fund returning 7% pre-tax taxed at equity rates (12.5% LTCG hold >1y) yields ~6.1% post-tax vs. Medium Term Debt at 6.7% post-tax. But if held <1 year, arbitrage is 20% STCG (~5.6% post-tax), still closer than debt's slab-taxed 6.7% if the equity-taxed wrapper is held >1 year.
   - **Plan should have compared post-tax returns of slab-taxed debt vs. equity-taxed hybrids for the 30% bracket.** It didn't.

2. **No ELSS / 80C Utilization:**
   - Investor profile lists "tax_bracket": "30_percent_slab" but does **not list 80C situation** (salary deductions, existing ELSS, unused 80C headroom).
   - Plan should ask: Does Tyrion have ₹1.5L unused 80C limit? If yes, why not allocate ₹1.5L to ELSS (e.g., Axis ELSS or Mirae Mid-cap ELSS) with 3-year lock-in?
   - ELSS gets (a) 80C deduction (₹1.5L/year), (b) equity tax rates (12.5% LTCG post-3 years). This is vastly superior to Medium Term debt (30% slab tax, no 80C).
   - Plan ignores this entirely — no mention of ELSS, no assumption on 80C headroom.

3. **Passive Mid-Cap Index (Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150):**
   - Correctly chosen at 0.2% fee (equity-taxed, ≥75% equities so 12.5% LTCG if held >1 year). This is tax-optimal for the 30% slab.
   - However, plan does not explicitly state: "I chose this index fund because at 0.2% fee + equity tax treatment (12.5% LTCG), it's post-tax efficient vs. active mid-caps (1.15% fee slab-taxed at 30%)."

4. **No Post-Tax Return Comparison in Plan Rationale:**
   - Fund rationales mention pre-tax returns (19.5% CAGR, 21.7% CAGR, etc.) but **never compare post-tax returns.**
   - Example: BoI Flexi Cap's 19.51% 5-year return becomes ~17.1% post-tax (LTCG 12.5% on gains >₹1.25L, assuming gains are >50% of return). Plan doesn't mention this.
   - AB Medium Term's 12.69% 5-year return becomes ~8.9% post-tax (30% slab). Plan does mention post-tax (6.7%) but only for the 1-year horizon, not 5-year compounding.

5. **Positive Elements:**
   - Plan correctly uses **direct plans** (not regular) — saves 0.5-1% in commissions, which is tax-efficient.
   - Plan correctly uses **growth option** (not dividend) — no capital gains tax on distributions; all gains taxed on redemption at favorable LTCG rates (if >1 year).
   - Rebalancing guideline mentions "Sell equities only after holding >1 year (you'll qualify for 12.5% LTCG on gains >₹1.25L)" — shows LTCG awareness.
   - Debt-first rebalancing ("Sell debt first, already slab-taxed") is smart: avoids realizing large equity gains.

**Summary:**
Plan is **tax-blind on the most critical choice: why slab-taxed debt instead of equity-taxed hybrids for a 30% investor.** This is a material flaw. For ₹250Cr lumpsum + ₹9k/month SIP = ~₹3.5-4Cr in Medium Term debt over 22 years, the post-tax return loss (slab-taxed vs. equity-taxed) could easily be ₹30-50L in forgone compounding.

Additionally, **ELSS + 80C is ignored entirely**, which is a major omission for a 30%-slab investor with ₹90k/month surplus (clearly has headroom for ₹1.5L ELSS annually).

**Tax Efficiency Score: 0.62** — Plan shows some tax awareness (direct, growth, LTCG mention, debt-first rebalancing) but **fails on the two biggest post-tax optimization levers: debt-vs.-hybrid trade-off for 30% slab, and ELSS/80C omission.** A truly tax-optimal plan would substitute Medium Term Debt with Aggressive Hybrid or arbitrage, and carve out ₹1.5L/year for ELSS.

---

## Summary Table

| Dimension | Score | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| **Goal Alignment** | 0.82 | Good but missing goal-specific projections and CFP-style proof. |
| **Diversification** | 0.78 | Solid domestic mix, but no international exposure (investor preference ignored) and no inflation hedges. |
| **Risk-Return Appropriateness** | 0.80 | Realistic base case and sound allocation, but lacks stress-testing and volatility transparency. |
| **Internal Consistency** | 0.85 | Strong narrative and setup, but minor breaks on debt purpose, international omission, SIP step-up assumption. |
| **Tax Efficiency** | 0.62 | **Critical gap:** slab-taxed debt chosen without justifying why it beats equity-taxed hybrids; ELSS/80C ignored entirely. |

**Composite PQS (unweighted avg): 0.774**

This is a **well-reasoned, operationally sound plan with strong execution details**, but it **underweights post-tax optimization** — a particularly glaring omission given the investor is in the 30% bracket and states "reads fund factsheets the way others read novels." Tyrion would likely spot the tax miss himself.

**Recommendation for improvement:**
1. Swap AB Medium Term Debt (₹250Cr lumpsum + ₹9k SIP) for **Motilal Oswal Aggressive Hybrid** or **ICICI Arbitrage Fund** (equity-taxed, 12.5% LTCG post-1 year).
2. Add **ELSS allocation**: ₹1.5L lumpsum + ₹12.5k/month SIP to fill 80C headroom (switch ₹9k from Medium Term Debt SIP to ELSS, add ₹3.5k from discretionary savings or reallocate from another fund).
3. Explicitly calculate and show **post-tax CAGR** for each fund under 30% slab taxation.
4. Address international exposure: add 5-10% to **Motilal Oswal Global Equity Fund** or **Franklin Templeworth Growth Fund (Equity)** to hedge education-abroad goal and INR risk.
5. Provide CFP-style **goal-to-portfolio projection**: show portfolio value at age 55, confirm ₹8Cr target is hit, and flag if SIP increase rate needs adjustment.
